title: `createContainer` hook cannot be run with `--userns=auto` or `--userns=nomap`
body:
### Issue Description

When Podman creates a container in a user namespace where the current user is not mapped, any OCI hook at the stage `createContainer` will fail to run.

The issue affects both privileged and rootless podman.

```
$ cat hooks/hook.json
{
	"version": "1.0.0",
	"hook": {
		"path": "/bin/true"
	},
	"when": {"always": true},
	"stages": ["createContainer"]
}
```

```
$ podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=nomap fedora:latest echo hi
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: {"msg":"error executing hook `/bin/true` (exit code: 1)","level":"error","time":"2025-12-01T14:04:03.199675Z"}
```

```
$ podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=host fedora:latest echo hi
hi
```

```
$ podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=keep-id fedora:latest echo hi
hi
```

```
$ sudo podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=auto fedora:latest echo hi
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: {"msg":"error executing hook `/bin/true` (exit code: 1)","level":"error","time":"2025-12-01T14:04:09.519875Z"}
```

```
$ sudo podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=host fedora:latest echo hi
hi
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Create a hook as described above.
2. Try to run a container with `--userns=nomap` or `--userns=auto`.
3. Observe that it fails to start.


### Describe the results you received

```
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: {"msg":"error executing hook `/bin/true` (exit code: 1)","level":"error","time":"2025-12-01T14:04:09.519875Z"}
```

### Describe the results you expected

The hook (`/bin/true`) runs successfully.

### podman info output

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Happy new year!

The issue is still present in Podman 5.7.1.

I stumbled upon something similar earlier this week, and my hunch is that this is an `crun` issue. AFAICT, `createContainer` gets passed along to the OCI runtime, and after precursory look at the code, I don't see anything that jumps out to me as Podman doing something wrong.
When I get a free sec, I'm curious to see if the same thing happens with `runc`.

```
[podman@8ed1b00431f3 ~]$ cat hooks/hooks.json
{
	"version": "1.0.0",
	"hook": {
		"path": "/bin/true"
	},
	"when": {"always": true},
	"stages": ["createContainer"]
}
```

With `runc` - 

```
[podman@8ed1b00431f3 ~]$ podman system info | grep runc
    name: runc
    package: runc-1.4.0-1
    path: /usr/bin/runc
      runc version 1.4.0
[podman@8ed1b00431f3 ~]$ podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=nomap alpine:edge echo hi
hi
[podman@8ed1b00431f3 ~]$ podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=host alpine:edge echo hi
hi
[podman@8ed1b00431f3 ~]$ podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=keep-id alpine:edge echo hi
hi
```

With `crun` - 

```
[podman@8ed1b00431f3 ~]$ podman system info | grep crun
    name: crun
    package: crun-1.26-3
    path: /usr/bin/crun
      crun version 1.26
      rundir: /tmp/storage-run-1000/crun
[podman@8ed1b00431f3 ~]$ podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=nomap alpine:edge echo hi
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: {"msg":"error executing hook `/bin/true` (exit code: 1)","level":"error","time":"2026-02-13T20:15:03.888165Z"}
[podman@8ed1b00431f3 ~]$ podman --hooks-dir=hooks run --rm --userns=host alpine:edge echo hi
hi
```

https://github.com/containers/crun/issues/1673 seems closely related.

Admittedly, I'm spitballing on what little I know about the spec - but from a quick glance, it seems underspecified here (around implicit hook working-directory behavior), so I don’t think this is a straightforward spec violation either way on `crun`'s part or `runc`'s part, but I don't know enough to say for sure.

I think a safer/less surprising behavior would be:

- keep a strict failure when `cwd` is explicitly configured, but inaccessible, and
- avoid forced implicit `chdir`for hooks when no `cwd` is explicitly requested

If `cwd` is implicit and a hook needs directory access, failure should happen when the hook actually touches that path. The current `chdir` before behavior causes an earlier failure, even for hooks that wouldn’t have needed to touch that directory at all.

(sorry, all of that commentary would make more sense over there than here, but I wanted to get it out of my head)

@hvenev-insait - I'd recommend bringing this issue up over in `crun` - you might get some more traction there.

I'm experiencing the same error with Podman 5.4.2, in Debian 13, when using rootless containers (I've not tested this issue with rootful containers yet).

In my case, I'm not using the `--userns` option but the `--uidmap` and `--gidmap` options. The reason is that I prefer to have a more precise control over the permissions in my shared volumes. Anyway, the workaround seems to be the same in both cases: **if you map the current user in the host to any user within the container, the error goes away.**

For example, if your UID in the host is `1000`, you can map it to the user `2000` in the container by passing the option `--userns='auto:uidmapping=2000:0:1,gidmapping=2000:0:1` (for rootless containers, `0` in the host UID is really an intermediate UID that equals the host UID `1000`) .

The idea is to choose, for the container-side, a dummy UID that is:

1. Unprivileged (so not `0`).
2. Different from the UID used to run the application.
3. Different from the dummy UID used in other container.

That way, if the app is compromised and the attacker escapes the namespace, your user in the host will still be safe (as long as there isn't ALSO a privilege escalation in the container and the attacker manages to run *whatever* as the dummy UID).
